coredata.dc:title: In-situ embedding of carbon dots in boron nitride for enhancing phosphor stability and fluorescent film heat dissipation
coredata.dc:creator: He, Lili
Yang, Li
Ge, Chunhua
Liu, Rui
Li, Wenxin
Zhang, Xiangdong
coredata.dc:description: The aggregation-induced quenching effect and poor thermal stability issues of carbon dots (CDs) limit their further development in the solid-state lighting and display field. In this work, a red carbon dot/boron nitride nanosheet (rCDs/BNNS) composite was synthesized using boron nitride as a carrier by in-situ embedding of rCDs. Thanks to the uniform dispersion of rCDs and the restraint of BNNS, the prepared rCDs/BNNS composite has excellent fluorescence stability and thermal stability. rCDs/BNNS composites as fluorescent markers can be used to detect potential fingerprints on various surfaces. Furthermore, rCDs/BNNS were dispersed into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) polymers to prepare rCDs/BNNS/PVA flexible luminescent films. In addition to the excellent stability inherited from rCDs/BNNS, the thin film also exhibits high heat dissipation by reason of the establishment of a heat transfer channel in the PVA by rCDs/BNNS. Moreover, thin films can still maintain original fluorescence after undergoing hundreds or thousands of bends, while possessing sufficient tensile strength. This study provides a facile pathway for the preparation of highly stable CDs luminescent materials, and has a promising application in the forensic identification and flexible luminescent devices' thermal management fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Boron nitride
Stability
Flexible films
Heat dissipation
Latent fingerprint detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermal management capability of rCDs/BNNS/PVA film
Latent fingerprints detection of rCDs/BNNS composite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Provincial
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Provincial Department of Education

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Provincial ( LJC201916 and LFW201901 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra of rCD, BNNS, rCDs/BNNS, PVA and rCDs/BNNS/PVA samples.
XRD patterns of BNNS, rCDs/BNNS, PVA and rCDs/BNNS/PVA samples.
SEM images of (a) BNNS, (b) rCDs/BNNS, (g) PVA and (h) rCDs/BNNS/PVA, respectively. TEM images of (c) BNNS, (d) rCDs, (e, f) rCDs/BNNS, and the insets are the HRTEM images of rCDs.

PL spectra of (a) rCDs/BNNS at 360–450 nm excitation wavelengths, (b, c) rCDs/BNNS (1–6) at 365 nm and 450 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively, (d) rCDs/BNNS/PVA, and (e) photographs of rCDs/BNNS, PVA and rCDs/BNNS/PVA samples.
Stability tests of samples, (a) in air, (b) UV-light, (c) under diverse temperature surroundings and (d) TGA curves.
(a) PL intensity retention after 1000 bends, and (b) tensile curves of rCDs/BNNS/PVA film, (c, d) Photos of flexible LED filaments wrapped in rCDs/BNNS/PVA film turning off and on in any state.
Infrared images of (a) rCDs/BNNS/PVA and (b) rCDs/PVA on LED chip. (c) Evolution of surface temperature of the LED chip versus time. (c) Heat transfer diagram of rCDs/BNNS/PVA film.
Latent fingerprints detection images taken under day-light and UV-light using rCDs/BNNS composites as the fluorescent labeling markers on the glass, ruler, coin and bottle caps.
Schematic illustration for preparation process of (a) rCDs/BNNS composites and (b) rCDs/BNNS/PVA composite films.

head.ce:title: In-situ embedding of carbon dots in boron nitride for enhancing phosphor stability and fluorescent film heat dissipation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang 110036, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang 110036, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aggregation-induced quenching effect and poor thermal stability issues of carbon dots (CDs) limit their further development in the solid-state lighting and display field. In this work, a red carbon dot/boron nitride nanosheet (rCDs/BNNS) composite was synthesized using boron nitride as a carrier by in-situ embedding of rCDs. Thanks to the uniform dispersion of rCDs and the restraint of BNNS, the prepared rCDs/BNNS composite has excellent fluorescence stability and thermal stability. rCDs/BNNS composites as fluorescent markers can be used to detect potential fingerprints on various surfaces. Furthermore, rCDs/BNNS were dispersed into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) polymers to prepare rCDs/BNNS/PVA flexible luminescent films. In addition to the excellent stability inherited from rCDs/BNNS, the thin film also exhibits high heat dissipation by reason of the establishment of a heat transfer channel in the PVA by rCDs/BNNS. Moreover, thin films can still maintain original fluorescence after undergoing hundreds or thousands of bends, while possessing sufficient tensile strength. This study provides a facile pathway for the preparation of highly stable CDs luminescent materials, and has a promising application in the forensic identification and flexible luminescent devices' thermal management fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention due to their low toxicity, low cost, and excellent optical properties, which endow CDs with potential application prospects, such as sensing, imaging, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), and other fields [1–3]. However, most solid CDs will undergo aggregation-induced quenching owing to resonance energy transfer or π-π interactions. Besides, solid CDs are also difficult to preserve because of strong water absorption. These defects gravely restrict its widespread use in solid-state lighting and display [4,5]. At present, researchers have invested much energy in the design, preparation, and performance modulation of CDs composites, and found that the adverse self-quenching effect can be successfully overcome by embedding CDs into a suitable solid matrix [6,7]. Nevertheless, another problem that cannot be ignored is the reduced thermal stability and luminescence activity due to changes in chemical bonds on the surface. In the case of CDs, their luminescent activity also decreases slightly at 80 °C [8–10]. LEDs are widely used in lighting and display devices in daily life. Studies have shown that when operating LED devices, the temperature of the phosphor layer may easily surpass 130 °C, significantly exceeding the thermal capacity limit of CDs. Therefore, solving the issues of thermal quenching is a major challenge to the CDs practical application.

The most direct and effective way to solve the above problems is to encapsulate CDs in a material with high thermal conductivity [11,12]. However, until now, there are few suitable thermally conductive materials to improve the heat-conducting properties of the light-emitting composite. Since commonly used thermally conductive substances, such as graphene and carbon nanotubes, are light absorbers, the luminescence properties of optical materials will inevitably be damaged even under a small load [13–15]. Reinforcing fillers suitable for use in optical materials require high thermal conductivity and high transparency to visible light. Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), known as "white graphene", has been proven to be the best candidate for improving the thermal conductivity of luminescent materials without sacrificing optical properties on account of its superior thermal conductivity, electrical insulation, and thermal stability, especially the negligibility of visible light [16–19]. In particular, when h-BN is exfoliated into two-dimensional (2D) boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS), it will have a larger specific surface area, providing more reaction sites for the luminescent center, and the close contact between the two enables efficient heat dissipation of the luminescent composite.

Nowadays, with the popularity of wearable products, there is a growing demand for low-cost and flexible LEDs in the market [20–22]. As one of the most important components of LEDs, flexible luminescent materials have the advantages of deformability and crack resistance that traditional phosphors do not have, so they have more practical application value in flexible optoelectronic devices [23–25]. The light-emitting polymer film composed of nanomaterials and polymers has the advantage of combining the superior performance of nanomaterials with the machinability and flexibility of polymers. It solves the problems of inconsistent LED light color, phosphor precipitation, and unfavorable LED heat dissipation in traditional dispensing processes, improves the thermal stability of phosphor, and greatly improves the light color quality of LED, providing a simple and promising design path for flexible luminescent materials that meet practical applications [26–28].

Herein, a mass-produced and inexpensive strategy is presented for the in situ introduction of rCDs during the process of exfoliating commercial h-BN by water vapor-assisted melting hydroxide, and successfully synthesized rCDs/BNNS composites with high fluorescence stability and thermal stability. In addition, rCDs/BNNS/PVA flexible luminescent films were prepared by coating rCDs/BNNS in PVA. The rCDs/BNNS and luminescent film has excellent stability in a variety of environmental conditions. And even more surprisingly, rCDs/BNNS/PVA films show splendid heat dissipation capabilities in LED lighting fixture applications. Moreover, after undergoing hundreds or thousands of bends, PL intensity can still be maintained. These results provide a convenient method for designing and manufacturing nanocomposites and flexible films with high stability, which can be used as promising materials for various applications.

Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) was provided by Aladin Co., Ltd., potassium hydroxide (KOH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), neutral red (C15H17ClN4), citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O), Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA-1799) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were analytically pure and untreated.

Based on previous studies, citric acid (1.5 g) and neutral red (2.1 mg) were dissolved in 5 mL of distilled water and hydrothermally reacted at 180 °C for 4 h. The red carbon dots (rCDs) aqueous solution was obtained after further purification [29]. For the synthesis of rCDs/BNNS samples, a certain amount of rCDs was dropped into 0.5 g h-BN and stirred evenly. After drying, a series of rCDs/h-BN mixtures were obtained for standby. Then, 1.42 g NaOH, 1.10 g KOH, and the rCDs/h-BN powder were evenly ground until no particle sense was found. Add a small amount of deionized water to continue grinding into a paste, put it into a 25 mL autoclave, and reacted at 180 °C for 2 h. Remove the solid product into 500 mL of NaOH solution (5.0 mol/L), ultrasonic processing 4 h. The suspension was washed alternately with distilled water and ethanol to neutralize and dried at 60 °C to obtain rCDs/BNNS composites. A series of rCDs/BNNS composites with different content of rCDs are named rCDs/BNNS (1–6), and the corresponding amount of added rCDs is 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 mg. For pure BNNS, the procedure was the same as above except that rCDs were not added to the h-BN powder.

0.8 g Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was dissolved in deionized water, heated at 95 °C, and stirred until completely dissolved. A series of rCDs/BNNS samples were added, and 30 min of water bath ultrasound was carried out. Then pour the mixture onto a flat, clean mold. After drying, rCDs/BNNS/PVA (1–4) composite film was obtained ( Scheme 1).

The rCDs/BNNS composite material was used as fluorescent labeling makers to detect latent fingerprint imaging by the classical powder dusting method. Before preparing fingerprints, volunteers' fingers were thoroughly washed with soapy water and blow dry. Then gently rub your finger on greasy areas of your nose, forehead, or hair, and press it on various substrate surfaces with appropriate pressure to collect latent fingerprint samples, and dry them for 1–2 min. The rCDs/BNNS composite was then sprayed onto the substrate and excess powder was carefully blown away with a rubber suction ball. Finally, the fingerprint was imaged using a smartphone under 365 nm UV light.

The surface group, crystal structure, and micro-morphology are characterized by FTIR (Nicolet AVATAR 360), XRD (AXIS D8), SEM (JSM-6711 F), and TEM (JEM-2100 F). PL spectrum was measured with a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4600). The weight loss was examined using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA 550). The thermal images are captured by an infrared camera (Fotric 220). The mechanical properties were measured by a microcomputer tensile testing machine (LGD 200).

The functional group composition of the samples was analyzed by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 1, the broadband of rCDs near 3453 and 3165 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration of O-H and N-H. Meanwhile, the absorption bands at 1733, 1584, 1406, and 1204 cm−1 were ascribed to CN/CO, CC, C-N, and C-O-C stretching vibration, respectively. It showed that there might be groups on the rCDs surface, like hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, etc. [30,31]. BNNS shows two strong absorption peaks at 1366 cm−1 and 815 cm−1, which are derived from the stretching vibration of B-N and the bending vibration of B-N-B. In addition, the absorption peak at 3438 cm−1 is likely created by the stretching vibration of O-H, which proves that some hydroxyl groups may be generated during the stripping process of BNNS [32]. Therefore, hydrogen bonds can be formed between the carboxyl/hydroxyl/amino of rCDs and the hydroxyl of BNNS during the synthesis of rCDs/BNNS. For PVA, the peak at 3380 cm−1 belongs to the O-H stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 2927 cm−1 is attributed to C-H stretching vibration. The peak of 1094 cm−1 belongs to C-O tensile vibration [33]. In rCDs/BNNS/PVA samples, the main characteristic peaks of rCDs/BNNS and PVA were both present, which proved the successful preparation of rCDs/BNNS/PVA film.

The crystal structure of samples was analyzed by XRD pattern, as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S1. rCDs have many very sharp diffraction peaks, indicating that the prepared rCDs are formed by organic points, leading to high crystallinity (Fig. S1) [34–36]. After loading rCDs, only the characteristic diffraction peak of BNNS was detected in rCDs/BNNS samples but did not show a clear rCDs diffraction peak. This may be due to the fact that the diffraction intensity of rCDs is very weak and the number of rCDs in rCDs/BNNS is limited. These results also further indicate that the rCDs/BNNS composites still maintain the basic structure of h-BN (PDF#34-0421), and the crystal phase of BNNS modified by rCDs has not changed significantly. A wide diffraction peak appears around 19.3° of PVA, revealing the amorphous structure of PVA [37]. The XRD pattern of the rCDs/BNNS/PVA shows the superposition of the wide diffraction peak of PVA and the strong crystal diffraction peak of rCDs/BNNS, indicating that rCDs/BNNS is fully mixed into the PVA substrate.

To further demonstrate the successful recombination of rCDs, BNNS, and PVA, the micromorphology of samples was observed by SEM and TEM. Pure BNNS has a sheet-like planar structure and a smooth surface ( Fig. 3a, c). Adding rCDs did not obviously change the initial shape of BNNS, which was difficult to directly observe in SEM images due to the very small size and a low mass load of rCDs (Fig. 3b). TEM images showed that the prepared rCDs were uniform spherical particles with an average diameter of about 3 nm (Fig. 3d). The lattice fringes of rCDs in the HRTEM image (illustration of Fig. 3d) are clearly visible. The lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the [100] crystal plane of graphene [38]. By TEM observation, it can be seen that the surface of rCDs/BNNS has become significantly rough, and locally enlarged images show that small circular black spots have evenly adhered to the BNNS surface. The lattice fringes of small black dots are clearly visible with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 3e, f), which further reveals the existence of rCDs and provides strong evidence for the perfect recombination of rCDs and BNNS. In addition, EDS-mapping analysis of rCDs/BNNS composites was also carried out, as shown in Fig. S2. The results show that B, N, C, and O contained in rCDs/BNNS are distributed uniformly in composites. Since BNNS is a distinctive two-dimensional nanosheet structure, it can offer adequate growth sites for rCDs [39].

SEM images of rCDs/BNNS/PVA were used to characterize the distribution of rCDs/BNNS in PVA and the degree of adhesion to the matrix. As can be seen from Fig. 3g and e, the surface of the individual PVA film is smooth and flat, and rCDs/BNNS are uniformly dispersed in PVA without any aggregation. There was also no significant interfacial separation between rCDs/BNNS and PVA, indicating a good interaction between rCDs/BNNS and PVA.

Fig. 4a shows the PL spectra of rCDs/BNNS samples. When the excitation wavelengths varied from 360 to 450 nm, the emission peak position is almost unchanged (around 575 nm). The properties of excited independent emission indicate that rCDs/BNNS has a uniform distribution of surface functional groups [40]. While the maximum emission wavelength of rCDs aqueous solution is at 636 nm (Fig. S3), the obvious emission peak blue shift of rCDs/BNNS is mainly attributed to the pH-dependent luminescence characteristics of rCDs, which may be caused by the protonation and deprotonation of the -COOH, -OH and -NH2 functional groups on rCDs surface [41,42]. Also, as the environment around the CDs changes, the PL emission may also undergo corresponding changes. The PL emission spectra of rCDs/BNNS samples with different amounts of rCDs were studied at 365 nm and 450 nm excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 4b and Fig. 4c. The analysis results indicate that the addition of rCDs has almost no influence on the position of the emission peak. However, as the concentration of rCDs increases in rCDs/BNNS samples, the matching PL intensity decreased slightly after a large increase. The step-up in the first stage luminescence intensity is mainly owing to the increase in the number of luminescent centers on the surface of rCDs/BNNS. The loss of luminescence intensity in the second stage may be attributed to the limited number of BNNS being unable to provide sufficient growth sites for rCDs, so that the distance between rCDs is decreased, resulting in the enhancement of agglomeration between the rCDs [43–45]. In this experiment, when the additional amount rCDs reached 3.8 wt %, the obtained rCDs/BNNS (4) sample had the strongest emission. Therefore, rCDs/BNNS (4) samples were selected for subsequent related studies.

To meet the application requirements of composite materials in different fields, rCDs/BNNS/PVA flexible composite films were prepared based on rCDs/BNNS composite materials as luminescent medium and PVA with high optical transparency and good film forming ability as a polymer matrix. Fig. 4d displays the PL spectra of rCDs/BNNS/PVA films with different amounts of rCDs/BNNS. The original PVA film has nearly no fluorescence under UV excitation. Compared with rCDs/BNNS powder, the emission wavelength of rCDs/BNNS/PVA film has no obvious change, indicating that the original optical properties of rCDs/BNNS are still maintained. It can be seen that the PL intensity of rCDs/BNNS/PVA films is concentration-dependent, and the fluorescence intensity increases with the increase of rCDs/BNNS content, but the increasing trend becomes smaller and smaller. This may be due to the aggregation between rCDs/BNNS [46,47].

Optical stability is an important criterion in the practical application of luminous materials. Therefore, the fluorescence stability and thermal stability of rCDs/BNNS and rCDs/BNNS/PVA in maintaining their PL intensity were studied, as detected in Fig. 5. After 35 days of exposure to the surrounding environment and 240 min of uninterrupted UV-light radiation, rCDs/BNNS composites reserves 92 % and 96 % of the initial PL strength, respectively, due to the favorable protection of BNNS against the uniformly doped rCDs in situ. For rCDs/BNNS/PVA film, after the same treatment, under the dual protection of PVA and BNNS, the direct contact between rCDs and air can be prevented as much as possible, and mitigate the impact of UV light radiation, and its emission stability remains at 94 % and 97 % of the original (Fig. 5a, b). In addition, rCDs/BNNS/PVA film and rCDs/BNNS powder display steady luminescent properties under 125 °C after heating for 6 h (Fig. 5c). Because BNNS is a splendid thermal conductive substance, it is conducive to relieving the thermal stress inside the composite, thus slowing the degradation and aggregation of the luminescence center under the action of thermal stress [48].

To further demonstrate the important role of BNNS in thermal stability, the thermal stability enhancement of the composites was evaluated by TGA. As revealed in Fig. 5d, it can be observed that the rCDs start to appear obvious weightlessness around 160 °C, and the total weightlessness is 93.6 %. However, there was no significant weight loss (less than 5 %) during the entire process of rCDs/BNNS, as BNNS has excellent thermal capacity and excellent thermal resistance, which forms a heat-resistant layer around rCDs, limiting the thermal movement of rCDs. The weight loss rate of rCDs/BNNS/PVA membranes before 225 °C was 4.3 %, mainly due to intermolecular and intramolecular dehydration of PVA substrates. The rapid degradation at 225–360 °C is mainly due to the removal of PVA side groups, while the maximum degradation of PVA molecular chains mainly occurs at 360–470 °C. Compared with pure PVA, the thermal stability of rCDs/BNNS/PVA films is significantly improved, which is mainly attributed to the high thermal stability of rCDs/BNNS fillers. In addition, the good compatibility of rCDs/BNNS filler with the PVA polymer is another factor to further enhances thermal stability.

Fig. 6 displays the luminescent properties of the film under deformation and mechanical flexibility. As shown in Fig. 6a, the rCDs/BNNS/PVA film has sufficient flexibility to bend from 0° to 180° without breaking. More importantly, after 1000 bending cycles, the PL strength of the film almost did not decrease. This largely depends on the good flexibility of the PVA itself. In addition, the tensile test results in Fig. 6b also prove that rCDs/BNNS/PVA composite film has good mechanical properties, with a tensile strength of up to 71.49 MPa. Furthermore, after the rCDs/BNNS/PVA fluorescent film is wound on the flexible LED filament with blue light emission, the filament can still be deformed freely, and the composite film emits bright white light under the excitation of blue light. These results indicate that rCDs/BNNS/PVA has a promising prospect for application in flexible lighting and display.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal management capability of rCDs/BNNS/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: Using rCDs/BNNS/PVA as a thermal interface material for commercial LEDs, its thermal management ability in practical applications was intuitively characterized by infrared thermal imaging, as shown in Fig. 7. It was observed that the working temperature continued to increase after the device was turned on. The results demonstrated that the LED surface temperature of rCDs/BNNS/PVA was much lower than that of rCDs/PVA with increasing working time, and the maximum temperature difference was 25.5 °C within 180 s. It confirms that boron nitride has a considerable contribution to heat transfer and dissipation in fluorescent films. Due to the excellent thermal conductivity of boron nitride, the tight connection of rCDs/BNNS in the PVA matrix creates an efficient heat transfer channel that can quickly extract heat and dissipate it into the environment, thereby reducing the operating temperature of the LED. According to reports, for every 10 °C decrease in the temperature of LED lamps, the service life can be increased by 50 % [49]. Therefore, the rCDs/BNNS/PVA film can keep the LED at a suitable operating temperature, prolong its life and improve luminous efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latent fingerprints detection of rCDs/BNNS composite
ce:section.ce:para: Latent fingerprints (LFPs) are the most common form of fingerprints, and LFPs left at actual crime scenes can greatly assist police personnel in screening potential criminals and obtaining fingerprint clues. Based on the advantages of rCDs/BNNS composites demonstrated above, their application in LFPs detection was explored. Fig. 8 shows the use of rCDs/BNNS materials to serve as fluorescent markers for fingerprint images on various common objects such as glass, rulers, coins, and bottle caps. LFPs on various objects in sunlight are invisible to the naked eye. After rCDs/BNNS composite powder is attached to the fingerprint residue, a clear and bright fingerprint luminescent image can be obtained immediately under ultraviolet light irradiation. Therefore, rCDs/BNNS composites may be an ideal substitute for traditional fluorescent colorants to achieve fast and simple LFP imaging.

Overall, a simple and feasible method has been developed for the synthesis of rCDs/BNNS composites with high fluorescence stability and thermal stability by confining luminescent CDs uniformly in situ into the BNNS matrix. Further, rCDs/BNNS was coated in PVA to obtain rCDs/BNNS/PVA flexible film. Except for inheriting the excellent stability of rCDs/BNNS, rCDs/BNNS has established an effective heat transfer channel in the PVA matrix, which endows rCDs/BNNS/PVA films with high heat dissipation. Furthermore, thin films can still maintain their original PL intensity after undergoing hundreds or thousands of bends, while possessing sufficient tensile strength. The research provides a simple method for the design and preparation of high-stability CDs fluorescent composites, and also provides a valuable reference for promoting CDs application in flexible lighting devices.

Lili He: Formal analysis, Data curation, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Li Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Visualization, Software. Chunhua Ge: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Rui Liu: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Visualization. Wenxin Li: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation. Xiangdong Zhang: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interests regarding the publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Provincial (LJC201916 and LFW201901).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.172269.

ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diversity and tailorability of photoelectrochemical properties of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Chen, Y. Liu, Z. Kang, Diversity and Tailorability of Photoelectrochemical Properties of Carbon Dots, Acc. Chem. Res. 55 (2022) 3110–3124.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots in solid-state: From nanostructures to functional devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ren, L. Stagi, P. Innocenzi, Fluorescent carbon dots in solid-state: From nanostructures to functional devices, Prog. Solid State Chem. 62 (2021) 100295.
Q. Xu, H. Cai, W. Li, M. Wu, Y. Wu, X. Gong, Carbon dot/inorganic nanomaterial composites, J. Mater. Chem. A 10 (2022) 14709–14731.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile one-pot synthesis of long-term thermal stable CDs@AlOOH toward white-light illumination

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Creation and stabilization of carbon dots in silica-confined compartments with high thermal stability

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrostatically assembled carbon dots/boron nitride nanosheet hybrid nanostructures for thermal quenching-resistant white phosphors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Cheng, T. Ye, H. Mao, Y. Wu, W. Jiang, C. Ban, Y. Yin, J. Liu, F. Xiu, W. Huang, Electrostatically assembled carbon dots/boron nitride nanosheet hybrid nanostructures for thermal quenching-resistant white phosphors, Nanoscale 12 (2020) 524–529.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible and foldable films of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA white light composite with high stability and heat dissipation
